lastPrompt: I now have the issue again, that the auth failed because the token expired but the git helper has it stored. I can't enter a new one, what's the official way to surface it up to the git helper that th…

content.text:
**The official mechanism is `git credential reject`** — git's credential protocol has three verbs: `fill` (get one), `approve` (worked, keep it), `reject` (failed, forget it). Helpers that store credentials respond to `reject` by erasing the matching entry. **To unblock yourself right now**, run: ```sh printf "protocol=https\nhost=8b04592ed74a5cce30d355b07276caf3.artifacts.cloudflare.net\n\n" | git credential reject ``` That's the URL component of your target. Add `username=...` on its own line if you want to scope it to a specific account (some helpers store per-user entries). The next `git credential fill` will then either prompt or pick up whatever fresh token your helper is configured to return. **Why git-sync didn't surface this automatically** Looking at `internal/auth/auth.go:69-72`, git-sync calls `git credential fill` to obtain credentials but never calls `git credential reject` (or `approve`) afterwards. So when the helper hands us a stale token, we use it, hit 401, and surface the error — but the helper never learns the token is bad, so the next run gets the same stale token back. This is missing-feature territory, not a bug in this branch. The fix is a small auth-side change: 1. Track which credentials came from `git credential fill` (vs. flags, env, Entire DB) 2. When a request returns 401 with credentials sourced from the helper, call `git credential reject` with the same protocol/host/username/password 3. Optionally call `git credential approve` on first success after a fill — convention, not strictly required for our use case The reject input has the same shape as `fill`, but includes the username and password we used: ``` protocol=https host=example.com username= password=

``` (blank line terminates) **Where to wire it in git-sync** The 401 surfaces in `httpError` (`internal/gitproto/smarthttp.go`). But the auth provenance lives up in `internal/auth/auth.go`. Cleanest plumbing: - `lookupGitCredential` returns a `func() error` (the "reject closure") alongside the username/password, capturing the input it sent to `fill` - The session-level code wraps the `AuthMethod` with that closure - On 401, the session calls the closure (which runs `git credential reject` for the same input) Worth doing? Yes — exactly because this scenario (expired token in helper, can't enter new one) recurs and is annoying. **But it's separate from the stale-pool / streaming fix on this branch**. I'd file it as its own change. Want me to: - **A.** Just answer the question; you'll handle the credential-reject feature later (immediate command above unblocks you now) - **B.** Add the credential-reject support as a new branch off `main` (clean PR, separate from the streaming work) - **C.** Add it to the current branch (couples two unrelated fixes — not recommended)

input.command:
gh pr create --base main --head soph/http-stale-pool-fix --title "Fix materialized push against CDN-fronted HTTP targets (Cloudflare)" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

- Diagnoses and fixes two independent bugs that broke materialized push against Cloudflare Artifacts (\`use of closed network connection\` mid-upload):
  1. **Stale keep-alive pool entry**: HTTPS info-refs and receive-pack ran ~tens of seconds apart in materialized push; the pooled TLS connection expired on Cloudflare's edge during that gap. Disabled keep-alives on git-sync's default transport (one extra TLS handshake per request, negligible against multi-MB transfers).
  2. **Mid-stream stall during delta selection**: go-git's encoder ran delta selection synchronously inside \`Encode()\`, producing no bytes for tens of seconds while the receive-pack body was open. CDN idle-write timeout fired. Fixed by using \`packfile.WithObjectSelector\` (landed in [go-git#2142](https://github.com/go-git/go-git/pull/2142)) to run selection up front and stream the write phase through \`io.Pipe\`.
- Adds two diagnostic env-var-gated tools that proved load-bearing during the investigation: \`GITSYNC_HTTP_TRACE=1\` (httptrace per-request lifecycle + outgoing-request dump with redacted Authorization).
- Adds in-place verbose-mode progress output for the materialized encode/write phases (\`selecting deltas, elapsed X\` → \`encoding pack: Y MB, elapsed Z\`).

## Commits

Each commit is scoped to a single change so the diagnostic story is rollback-friendly:

1. \`d8a071d9\` httptrace env var — debugging tool, kept
2. \`a3a96da\` disable HTTP keep-alives — fixes the stale-pool race
3. \`6f1f5944\` outgoing-request dump under trace — debugging tool, kept
4. \`f76e2308\` spool materialized push body — the original fix (temp-file workaround for the stall)
5. \`b22b2840\` pack-encode progress — UX for the long materialized phase
6. \`05be962e\` SpooledBody encapsulation + doc trims — post-review cleanup
7. \`8aa0dab7\` switch to go-git \`WithObjectSelector\` — replaces the temp-file spool with proper streaming, now that go-git#2142 is merged

Commits 4 and 7 tell the iteration: spool first (real workaround), then streaming (proper fix) once the upstream API was available.

## Verification

- Reproduced original failure against Cloudflare Artifacts target (\`use of closed network connection\` at ~7.9 KB sent).
- Confirmed stale-pool diagnosis via \`GITSYNC_HTTP_TRACE=1\` — saw \`reused=true wasIdle=true idle=13.001639584s\` immediately before the failure.
- Confirmed mid-stream stall via \`GITSYNC_FORCE_CHUNKED=1\` experiment (temporary toggle since reverted): spooled body + chunked still succeeded, ruling out chunked encoding as the cause and isolating the stall.
- Final commit (streaming via \`WithObjectSelector\`) confirmed working end-to-end against Cloudflare Artifacts.
- All existing tests pass; \`go vet\` and \`gofmt -l\` clean.

## Test plan
- [x] \`go test ./...\` passes
- [x] Manual: \`sync --all-refs --progress\` against Cloudflare Artifacts target succeeds
- [x] Manual: \`GITSYNC_HTTP_TRACE=1\` output confirms chunked encoding (no Content-Length on receive-pack POST) and no stale-pool reuse
- [x] Manual: verbose-mode progress shows \`selecting deltas\` → \`encoded pack\` phase transition

## Open items

- \`go.mod\` pins go-git to the merge commit of go-git#2142 (pseudo-version \`v6.0.0-alpha.4.0.20260521151600-590487407c38\`). Switch to a tagged release once go-git cuts \`v6.0.0-alpha.5\`.
EOF

content.text: PR #64 opened, branch is now `soph/credential-reject-on-401` off latest main. Let me lay out the credential-reject plan before coding.

Plan for the credential-reject feature:

**Design**
- Add an optional `Resettable` interface (`Reset(ctx) error`) — `Method`s sourced from `git credential fill` implement it; others don't
- Wrap the git-credential-helper auth in a struct that captures the same input we sent to `fill`, so we can replay it as `reject` later
- In `RequestInfoRefs` and `PostRPCStreamBody`, when the response is 401 *and* `c.Auth` implements `Resettable`, call `Reset` — which shells out to `git credential reject`
- Reset-only, no auto-retry (V1). User reruns; second run sees the helper prompt for a new credential. Safer for CI; can add retry-and-prompt later if needed

This keeps the existing `Resolve`/`Method` API intact (purely additive interface) and only the git-credential path is affected.

Let me implement.

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
auth, gitproto: invalidate stale credential helper entries on 401

Resolve previously called git credential fill to obtain a credential,
used it for subsequent requests, and returned an error on 401 — but
never told the credential helper the credential it handed us was bad.
The helper kept serving the same stale entry on every retry, so the
user had to manually run `git credential reject` to unblock.

Wire git credential reject in so the helper learns when a credential
fails:

  - New auth.Resettable interface (optional, single method
    Reset(ctx) error). Methods sourced from git credential fill
    implement it; explicit-flag/Entire-DB methods don't.
  - gitCredentialHelperAuth wraps a Method and captures the same
    fill input plus the username/password we used so it can replay
    them as reject input.
  - gitproto.maybeResetAuthOn401 type-asserts c.Auth against the
    resettable shape (duck-typed to avoid an import cycle) and calls
    Reset when the response status is exactly 401. Wired into both
    RequestInfoRefs and PostRPCStreamBody.

V1 is reset-only: the failing request still returns the 401 error.
The next run looks up afresh — helpers prompt for new credentials
after a reject — so the typical workflow becomes "first run fails
loudly, second run succeeds." We don't auto-retry-and-prompt inside
the same run because git-sync is also used non-interactively in CI
where a sudden interactive prompt would surprise.

Tests cover:
  - credentialRejectInput shape (matches fill plus username/password)
  - gitCredentialHelperAuth.Reset invokes the credential reject
    command with the right input, and wraps errors from it
  - maybeResetAuthOn401 calls Reset on 401, not on other statuses,
    handles non-resettable AuthMethods, and tolerates nil
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -3

content.text:
Done. Status of all three asks:

**1. PR opened** — https://github.com/entireio/git-sync/pull/64 — branch `soph/http-stale-pool-fix` with the full streaming-fix story (7 commits).

**2. go-git version** — pinned to the merge commit via pseudo-version (`v6.0.0-alpha.4.0.20260521151600-590487407c38`). No new alpha cut yet on go-git's side; will bump to a tagged release when one drops.

**3. Credential-reject branch** — `soph/credential-reject-on-401` off latest main, one commit (`c750c96b`).

**On the credential-reject branch:**

- **`internal/auth`** — `Resettable` interface (optional, single `Reset(ctx) error` method). `gitCredentialHelperAuth` wraps the helper-sourced auth, captures the reject input, and shells out to `git credential reject` via the new `GitCredentialRejectCommand` hook (mirrors `GitCredentialFillCommand` for testability).
- **`internal/gitproto`** — `maybeResetAuthOn401` type-asserts the conn's `AuthMethod` against the duck-typed resettable shape (no import cycle). Wired into both `RequestInfoRefs` and `PostRPCStreamBody` so 401s on either info-refs or upload-pack/receive-pack POSTs invalidate the cached credential.
- **V1 scope** — reset only, no auto-retry inside the same run. Rationale in the commit message: CI safety. Easy to add retry-and-prompt later if interactive UX becomes the priority.
- **Tests** — covering reject-input shape, the Reset shell-out, error wrapping, and that `maybeResetAuthOn401` no-ops correctly on non-401 statuses, non-resettable auths, and nil.

Push & PR for this one whenever you're ready — `git push -u origin soph/credential-reject-on-401` + `gh pr create`. I held off because it's a separate piece of work and I wanted you to read the design first.

content.content:
--- Author: cursor[bot] File: internal/gitproto/push.go:237 Body: ### Success progress on selection failure **Medium Severity** In `PushObjects`, the `stopSelect` progress message is finalized before checking for errors from `ObjectsToPack`. This means a "selected N objects" message can appear even when delta selection fails, which is misleading for operators.
 
Reviewed by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/bugbot) for commit 8aa0dab7f17c413efd27765509bfcb3fccad6196. Configure [here](https://www.cursor.com/dashboard/bugbot). --- Author: cursor[bot] File: internal/gitproto/push.go:248 Body: ### Concurrent progress writer use **Medium Severity** In verbose mode, `startPackWriteProgress` writes to `conn.ProgressWriter()` from the encode goroutine while `sendReceivePack` streams target sideband progress through another `progressSink` on the same underlying writer (often `sessionStderr`). That writer is stateful and not synchronized, so overlapping writes can corrupt buffered lines and scramble the live progress UI.
Additional Locations (1) - [`internal/gitproto/push.go#L170-L176`](https://github.com/entireio/git-sync/blob/8aa0dab7f17c413efd27765509bfcb3fccad6196/internal/gitproto/push.go#L170-L176)

37	// httpError checks an HTTP response status and returns an error for non-2xx responses.
38	func httpError(res *http.Response) error {
39		if res.StatusCode >= http.StatusOK && res.StatusCode < http.StatusMultipleChoices {
40			return nil
41		}
42		var reason string
43		if res.Body != nil {
44			limited := io.LimitReader(res.Body, maxHTTPErrorBody+1)
45			data, err := io.ReadAll(limited)
46			if err == nil && len(data) > 0 {
47				if len(data) > maxHTTPErrorBody {
48					data = append(data[:maxHTTPErrorBody], []byte("...")...)
49				}
50				reason = strings.TrimSpace(string(data))
51			}
52		}
53		var diag []string
54		for _, h := range diagnosticHeaders {
55			if v := res.Header.Get(h); v != "" {
56				diag = append(diag, h+"="+v)
57			}
58		}
59		if len(diag) > 0 {
60			return fmt.Errorf("http %d: %s [%s] %s", res.StatusCode, res.Request.URL.Redacted(), strings.Join(diag, ", "), reason)
61		}
62		return fmt.Errorf("http %d: %s %s", res.StatusCode, res.Request.URL.Redacted(), reason)
63	}

131		return httptrace.WithClientTrace(ctx, trace)
132	}

134	// dumpOutgoingRequest prints the wire-format request line and headers for
135	// req to stderr, prefixed with label. The body is not consumed (passes
136	// body=false to httputil.DumpRequestOut), but Transfer-Encoding and
137	// Content-Length will reflect what Go's transport would actually send.
138	// Useful when a server behaves unexpectedly on a POST and you need to
139	// see what the request looked like at the protocol level — the
140	// connection-level trace tells you which TCP/TLS connection was used
141	// but not what was written on it. Best-effort: dump errors are
142	// surfaced as a single line so a transient dump failure doesn't mask
143	// the underlying request.
144	func dumpOutgoingRequest(req *http.Request, label string) {
145		dump, err := httputil.DumpRequestOut(req, false)
146		if err != nil {
147			fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "[httptrace] %s dump error: %v\n", label, err)
148			return
149		}
150		fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "[httptrace] %s outgoing request:\n%s\n", label, redactAuthorization(dump))
151	}
152	
153	// redactAuthorization scrubs any Authorization header value from a dumped
154	// HTTP request so the credentials don't leak into stderr when
155	// GITSYNC_HTTP_TRACE is enabled in environments with shoulder-surfers,
156	// pasted-into-tickets logs, or shared shells.
157	func redactAuthorization(dump []byte) []byte {
158		const header = "Authorization:"
159		idx := bytes.Index(dump, []byte(header))
160		if idx < 0 {
161			return dump
162		}
163		end := bytes.IndexByte(dump[idx:], '\n')
164		if end < 0 {
165			end = len(dump) - idx
166		}
167		out := make([]byte, 0, len(dump))
168		out = append(out, dump[:idx]...)
169		out = append(out, []byte(header+" [REDACTED]")...)
170		out = append(out, dump[idx+end:]...)
171		return out
172	}

295	func (cw *countingWriter) Count() int64 { return cw.n.Load() }
296	
297	// startSelectionProgress emits in-place "selecting deltas, elapsed X"
298	// updates every 500ms during the synchronous delta-selection phase of
299	// PushObjects. The returned stop function takes the number of selected
300	// objects and finalizes the line with a permanent "selected N objects
301	// in Y" summary. When dest is nil (non-verbose mode) returns a no-op
302	// stop, so callers don't need to special-case verbosity.
303	//
304	// Selection has no observable byte progress — go-git's DeltaSelector
305	// is opaque to the caller — so elapsed time is the only signal we can
306	// surface to keep long selections from looking like a hang.
307	func startSelectionProgress(dest io.Writer) func(objectCount int) {
308		if dest == nil {
309			return func(int) {}
310		}
311		start := time.Now()
312		ticker := time.NewTicker(500 * time.Millisecond)
313		stop := make(chan struct{})
314		done := make(chan struct{})
315		go func() {
316			defer close(done)
317			for {
318				select {
319				case <-stop:
320					return
321				case <-ticker.C:
322					fmt.Fprintf(dest, "selecting deltas, elapsed %s\r",
323						time.Since(start).Round(time.Second))
324				}
325			}
326		}()
327		return func(objectCount int) {
328			ticker.Stop()
329			close(stop)
330			<-done
331			fmt.Fprintf(dest, "selected %d objects in %s\n",
332				objectCount, time.Since(start).Round(time.Second))
333		}
334	}
335

338	// cw. The returned stop function finalizes the line with a permanent
339	// "encoded pack" summary. Single-use, typically via defer. When dest
340	// is nil returns a no-op stop.
341	//
342	// This is the second of two phases visible to a materialized push:
343	// startSelectionProgress runs synchronously first, then
344	// startPackWriteProgress takes over once selection has completed and

content.text: Commit and push:

input.command:
git add internal/gitproto/push.go internal/syncer/progress.go && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
gitproto, syncer: address PR #64 review feedback

Three small fixes after Bugbot review:

- gitproto/push.go countingWriter.Write: wrap the underlying write
  error so wrapcheck stops failing the lint job (CI run 26288151165).

- gitproto/push.go PushObjects: pass the selection error into the
  stopSelect callback. Previously "selected N objects in X" was
  printed even when ObjectsToPack returned an error, which was
  misleading (count was 0 or partial, and the next line was the
  actual error). startSelectionProgress now suppresses the success
  summary when err != nil; the ticker still stops cleanly either way.

- syncer/progress.go sessionStderr: serialize Write under a mutex.
  The materialized-push refactor in this PR runs an encode-progress
  ticker concurrently with sendReceivePack, and both write to the
  same conn.ProgressWriter() (which is *sessionStderr). The
  strings.Builder buffer and the progress notify/setTransient calls
  weren't synchronized, so overlapping writes could scramble the
  live progress UI or, in theory, corrupt the buffer.
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -3 && git push 2>&1 | tail -3
